content:
Hi, I'm trying to auto-generate a comment in a macro_rules! call, but I can't seem to do it in a way that would be legible because it appears for some reason we're missing format!?

            #[doc = format!(
                r#"Convert a [`{opaque}`] into its fully qualified [`{full}`].

                This is an infallible version of [`{full}::try_from_opaque`]. This function is \
                mainly useful for drivers where only a single [`{drv_trait}`] implementation \
                exists.

                # Panics

                This function will panic if the underlying [`{full}`] in the provided [`{opaque}`] \
                does not belong to the same [`{drv_trait}`] implementation."#,
                opaque = stringify!($opaque),
                full = stringify!($full),
                drv_trait = stringify!($drv_trait)
            )]
            pub fn from_opaque<'a, D>(opaque: &'a $opaque<D>) -> &'a $full<T>
            where
                D: KmsDriver,
                T: $drv_trait<Driver = D>
            {
                Self::try_from_opaque(opaque)
                    .expect(concat!("Passed ", stringify!($opaque), "does not share this ",
                                    stringify!($drv_trait), " implementation."))
            }

doesn't work for this reason. What is the equivalent I should be using here?
I'm realizing that this would be format_args!(), but it seems doc doesn't like that because it's not a literal :(

you should be able to use concat! instead (and also normal doc parts for the stuff that doesn't require interpolation):

#[doc = concat!("Convert a [`", stringify!($opaque), "`] into its fully qualified [`", stringify!($full), "`]")]
///
#[doc = concat!("This is an infallible version of [`", stringify!($full), "::try_from_opaque`]. This function is")]
...
/// # Panics
///
...
I mean I can. it's just a lot more difficult to read lol
Ah, I thought you didn't know about that :)
writing docs for generated code in macro_rules! is sadly rather painful...
yeah, it would be alright if we had a equivalent of format!() that we could use instead of concat!()
